narrative: As an Asian male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical 3.21 average for students in our dataset—and an LSAT score of 31, which is below the average of about 37. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 72.5% chance that you would fail the bar exam, tipping the scales toward rejection of your application. This prediction hinges on how your academic trajectory stacks up against others who went on to take the bar. The most influential factor pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year—far below the average decile of about 5.5—which signaled to the model a concerning dip in performance relative to students who typically pass. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile of 1, again in the lowest tenth compared to the average around 5.7, reinforcing a pattern of early underperformance that the model associates strongly with bar struggles. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA actually pulled the prediction slightly toward success, as it aligned closely with the strong GPAs of those who passed, offering some counterbalance. Compared to typical admitted students, who often rank higher in school deciles and score better on the LSAT, your early grades were the standout hurdles that outweighed these strengths. While your full-time status matches the vast majority at 93%, it didn't sway the outcome much here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male from a supportive background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through stronger coursework or prep in future terms, retake the LSAT to aim above the 37 average, and consider part-time options or gap-year experiences that demonstrate upward trends. Gathering letters of recommendation highlighting your resilience could also help reframe your story for other schools. With deliberate steps like these, you can reshape how models and admissions teams see your path to the bar.
